It is believed to have originally been a day when a {{ill|bride|ja|嫁|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bride|qq=}} in a {{ill|virilocal marriage|ja|嫁取り婚|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/virilocal marriage|qq=}} would return to her parents' home. However, as urbanization progressed, it evolved into a custom for apprentices and domestic workers<ref name="heibon">「国民百科事典7」平凡社 p292-293 1962nd year 6月15日初版発行</ref>.
In some regions, particularly in {{ill|Kansai|ja|関西地方|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai|qq=}} and [[:en:Kagoshima (disambiguation)|Kagoshima]], Yabuiri was known as '''O-yagenzo''' ('''親見参''', "Parent Visitation")<ref name="heibon" /><ref name="koubunkan">「日本民俗大辞典 下」p737 吉川弘文館 2000th year 3月10日1版発行</ref>. Since the holiday was observed on days ending in six, it was also referred to as '''Rokuiri''' ('''六入り''', "Six-entry") in Kansai<ref name="koubunkan" />.
The dates of {{ill|Lunisolar January 15|ja|1月15日 (旧暦)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lunisolar January 15|qq=}} ([[:en:First Full Moon Festival|Koshōgatsu]], the "Little New Year") and {{ill|Lunisolar July 15|ja|7月15日 (旧暦)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lunisolar July 15|qq=}} ([[:en:Obon|Obon]], the Festival of the Dead) were important festival days. The timing of Yabuiri allowed apprentices and maids to complete their duties at their workplaces before returning home to take part in family festivities<ref name="koubunkan" />. Over time, these dates also became associated with the belief that {{ill|Enma Daio|ja|閻魔大王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enma Daio|qq=}}, the King of Hell, would cease punishing the souls of the dead on these days. As a result, many {{ill|Enma halls|ja|閻魔堂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enma halls|qq=}} and {{ill|Jūō halls|ja|十王堂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jūō halls|qq=}} would open their doors for special ceremonies, and [[:en:Ennichi|temple fairs]] would be held<ref name="koubunkan" />.
== Evolution == On Yabuiri, the master of the household would give his servants gifts such as new {{ill|kimono|ja|着物|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kimono|qq=}}, [[:en:Footwear|footwear]], spending money, and souvenirs to take home<ref name="koubunkan" />. The servants would then return to their family homes to spend the day with their parents. For those who had traveled long distances for work or had reached adulthood and could not return home, the day was spent enjoying entertainment such as {{ill|kabuki|ja|芝居|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kabuki|qq=}} performances or shopping.
Since Yabuiri was closely tied to the New Year and Obon festivals, it continued to be observed after the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], even after the [[:en:Lunar calendar|Japanese calendar]] was replaced by the [[:en:Solar calendar|Solar calendar]]. As Japan industrialized, the number of workers increased, making Yabuiri an even more significant event. On Yabuiri, bustling districts such as [[:en:Asakusa|Asakusa]] were crowded with apprentices and workers enjoying their rare day off. The growing popularity of [[:en:Film|motion pictures]] was also fueled by Yabuiri<ref name="koubunkan" />.
After [[:en:World War II|World War II]], changes in labor laws such as the strengthening of the [[:en:Labor Standards Act (Japan)|Labour Standards Act]] and the adoption of the {{ill|five-day workweek|ja|週休2日制|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/five-day workweek|qq=}} led to the decline of Yabuiri. Instead, it was absorbed into the longer {{ill|New Year holidays|ja|正月休み|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Year holidays|qq=}} and {{ill|Obon holidays|ja|盆休み|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Obon holidays|qq=}}, surviving mainly as the tradition of {{ill|returning home for the holidays|ja|帰省|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/returning home for the holidays|qq=}}.
== Etymology and Cultural Influence == The origin of the term ''Yabuiri'' is unclear, and several theories exist. One theory suggests that the name comes from the idea of returning to a "deep thicket" (藪, ''yabu'') in the countryside. Another suggests that it is derived from a corruption of ''Yado-iri'' (宿入り), meaning "returning to one's lodging"<ref name="koubunkan" /><ref>「日本人のしきたり」p55 飯倉晴武著 青春出版社 2003rd year 1月25日1版発行</ref>. A similar term, ''Yado-sagari'' (宿下がり), was used to describe women of the [[:en:Ōoku|Ōoku]] returning home.
Yabuiri was a rare and precious holiday for servants, making it a major event in their lives. As a result, the custom was frequently referenced in literature, [[:en:Haiku|haiku]], and [[:en:Rakugo|Rakugo]] stories such as {{ill|Yabuiri (Rakugo)|ja|藪入り|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yabuiri (Rakugo)|qq=}}, formerly known as {{ill|Okama-sama|ja|お釜さま|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okama-sama|qq=}}.

revision.comment: Bot: remove deleted category Japanese annual events
revision.text:
{{Lunisolar July}} {{Lunisolar January}} {{Otheruses|Yabuiri|落語|{{ill|Yabuiri (Rakugo)|ja|藪入り (落語)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yabuiri (Rakugo)|qq=}}}}
'''Yabuiri''' (藪入り) or '''Yabu-iri''' was a traditional holiday in Japan during which {{ill|apprentices|ja|丁稚|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/apprentices|qq=}} and {{ill|maids|ja|女中|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/maids|qq=}} who worked as live-in servants in {{ill|merchant houses|ja|商家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/merchant houses|qq=}} were allowed to return home to visit their families. The holiday was observed on {{ill|Lunisolar January 16|ja|1月16日 (旧暦)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lunisolar January 16|qq=}} and {{ill|Lunisolar July 16|ja|7月16日 (旧暦)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lunisolar July 16|qq=}}. The July observance was also called "{{ill|Later Yabuiri|ja|後の藪入り|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Later Yabuiri|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" (''Nochi no Yabuiri'')<ref>{{kotobank|後の藪入り}}</ref>.
== Origins == The custom of Yabuiri became widespread among urban merchant families during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. It is believed to have originally been a day when a {{ill|bride|ja|嫁|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bride|qq=}} in a {{ill|virilocal marriage|ja|嫁取り婚|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/virilocal marriage|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} would return to her parents' home. However, as urbanization progressed, it evolved into a custom for apprentices and domestic workers<ref name="heibon">「国民百科事典7」平凡社 p292-293 1962nd year 6月15日初版発行</ref>.
In some regions, particularly in {{ill|Kansai|ja|関西地方|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 関西}} and [[:en:Kagoshima (disambiguation)|Kagoshima]], Yabuiri was known as '''O-yagenzo''' ('''親見参''', "Parent Visitation")<ref name="heibon" /><ref name="koubunkan">「日本民俗大辞典 下」p737 吉川弘文館 2000th year 3月10日1版発行</ref>. Since the holiday was observed on days ending in six, it was also referred to as '''Rokuiri''' ('''六入り''', "Six-entry") in Kansai<ref name="koubunkan" />.
The dates of {{ill|Lunisolar January 15|ja|1月15日 (旧暦)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lunisolar January 15|qq=}} ([[:en:First Full Moon Festival|Koshōgatsu]], the "Little New Year") and {{ill|Lunisolar July 15|ja|7月15日 (旧暦)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lunisolar July 15|qq=}} ([[:en:Obon|Obon]], the Festival of the Dead) were important festival days. The timing of Yabuiri allowed apprentices and maids to complete their duties at their workplaces before returning home to take part in family festivities<ref name="koubunkan" />. Over time, these dates also became associated with the belief that {{ill|Enma Daio|ja|閻魔大王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enma Daio|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 閻魔}}, the King of Hell, would cease punishing the souls of the dead on these days. As a result, many {{ill|Enma halls|ja|閻魔堂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enma halls|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Jūō halls|ja|十王堂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jūō halls|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 正覚寺 (奈良市西紀寺町)}} would open their doors for special ceremonies, and [[:en:Ennichi|temple fairs]] would be held<ref name="koubunkan" />.
== Evolution == On Yabuiri, the master of the household would give his servants gifts such as new {{ill|kimono|ja|着物|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kimono|qq=}}, [[:en:Footwear|footwear]], spending money, and souvenirs to take home<ref name="koubunkan" />. The servants would then return to their family homes to spend the day with their parents. For those who had traveled long distances for work or had reached adulthood and could not return home, the day was spent enjoying entertainment such as {{ill|kabuki|ja|芝居|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kabuki|qq=}} performances or shopping.
Since Yabuiri was closely tied to the New Year and Obon festivals, it continued to be observed after the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], even after the [[:en:Lunar calendar|Japanese calendar]] was replaced by the [[:en:Solar calendar|Solar calendar]]. As Japan industrialized, the number of workers increased, making Yabuiri an even more significant event. On Yabuiri, bustling districts such as [[:en:Asakusa|Asakusa]] were crowded with apprentices and workers enjoying their rare day off. The growing popularity of [[:en:Film|motion pictures]] was also fueled by Yabuiri<ref name="koubunkan" />.
After [[:en:World War II|World War II]], changes in labor laws such as the strengthening of the [[:en:Labor Standards Act (Japan)|Labour Standards Act]] and the adoption of the {{ill|five-day workweek|ja|週休2日制|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/five-day workweek|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 休日}} led to the decline of Yabuiri. Instead, it was absorbed into the longer {{ill|New Year holidays|ja|正月休み|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Year holidays|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 正月}} and {{ill|Obon holidays|ja|盆休み|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Obon holidays|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to お盆}}, surviving mainly as the tradition of {{ill|returning home for the holidays|ja|帰省|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/returning home for the holidays|qq=}}.
== Etymology and Cultural Influence == The origin of the term ''Yabuiri'' is unclear, and several theories exist. One theory suggests that the name comes from the idea of returning to a "deep thicket" (藪, ''yabu'') in the countryside. Another suggests that it is derived from a corruption of ''Yado-iri'' (宿入り), meaning "returning to one's lodging"<ref name="koubunkan" /><ref>「日本人のしきたり」p55 飯倉晴武著 青春出版社 2003rd year 1月25日1版発行</ref>. A similar term, ''Yado-sagari'' (宿下がり), was used to describe women of the [[:en:Ōoku|Ōoku]] returning home.
Yabuiri was a rare and precious holiday for servants, making it a major event in their lives. As a result, the custom was frequently referenced in literature, [[:en:Haiku|haiku]], and [[:en:Rakugo|Rakugo]] stories such as {{ill|Yabuiri (Rakugo)|ja|藪入り|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yabuiri (Rakugo)|qq=}}, formerly known as {{ill|Okama-sama|ja|お釜さま|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okama-sama|qq=}}.
== Related Topics == * [[:en:Boxing Day|Boxing Day]]

revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11625091]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Lunisolar July}} {{Lunisolar January}} {{Otheruses|Yabuiri|落語|{{ill|Yabuiri (Rakugo)|ja|藪入り (落語)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yabuiri (Rakugo)|qq=}}}}
'''Yabuiri''' (藪入り) or '''Yabu-iri''' was a traditional holiday in Japan during which {{ill|apprentices|ja|丁稚|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/apprentices|qq=}} and {{ill|maids|ja|女中|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/maids|qq=}} who worked as live-in servants in {{ill|merchant houses|ja|商家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/merchant houses|qq=}} were allowed to return home to visit their families. The holiday was observed on {{ill|Lunisolar January 16|ja|1月16日 (旧暦)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lunisolar January 16|qq=}} and {{ill|Lunisolar July 16|ja|7月16日 (旧暦)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lunisolar July 16|qq=}}. The July observance was also called "{{ill|Later Yabuiri|ja|後の藪入り|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Later Yabuiri|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" (''Nochi no Yabuiri'')<ref>{{kotobank|後の藪入り}}</ref>.
== Origins == The custom of Yabuiri became widespread among urban merchant families during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. It is believed to have originally been a day when a {{ill|bride|ja|嫁|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bride|qq=}} in a {{ill|virilocal marriage|ja|嫁取り婚|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/virilocal marriage|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} would return to her parents' home. However, as urbanization progressed, it evolved into a custom for apprentices and domestic workers<ref name="heibon">「国民百科事典7」平凡社 p292-293 1962nd year 6月15日初版発行</ref>.
In some regions, particularly in {{ill|Kansai|ja|関西地方|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai|qq=|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=関西}} and [[:en:Kagoshima (disambiguation)|Kagoshima]], Yabuiri was known as '''O-yagenzo''' ('''親見参''', "Parent Visitation")<ref name="heibon" /><ref name="koubunkan">「日本民俗大辞典 下」p737 吉川弘文館 2000th year 3月10日1版発行</ref>. Since the holiday was observed on days ending in six, it was also referred to as '''Rokuiri''' ('''六入り''', "Six-entry") in Kansai<ref name="koubunkan" />.
The dates of {{ill|Lunisolar January 15|ja|1月15日 (旧暦)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lunisolar January 15|qq=}} ([[:en:First Full Moon Festival|Koshōgatsu]], the "Little New Year") and {{ill|Lunisolar July 15|ja|7月15日 (旧暦)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lunisolar July 15|qq=}} ([[:en:Obon|Obon]], the Festival of the Dead) were important festival days. The timing of Yabuiri allowed apprentices and maids to complete their duties at their workplaces before returning home to take part in family festivities<ref name="koubunkan" />. Over time, these dates also became associated with the belief that {{ill|Enma Daio|ja|閻魔大王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enma Daio|qq=|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=閻魔}}, the King of Hell, would cease punishing the souls of the dead on these days. As a result, many {{ill|Enma halls|ja|閻魔堂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enma halls|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Jūō halls|ja|十王堂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jūō halls|qq=|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=正覚寺 (奈良市西紀寺町)}} would open their doors for special ceremonies, and [[:en:Ennichi|temple fairs]] would be held<ref name="koubunkan" />.
== Evolution == On Yabuiri, the master of the household would give his servants gifts such as new {{ill|kimono|ja|着物|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kimono|qq=}}, [[:en:Footwear|footwear]], spending money, and souvenirs to take home<ref name="koubunkan" />. The servants would then return to their family homes to spend the day with their parents. For those who had traveled long distances for work or had reached adulthood and could not return home, the day was spent enjoying entertainment such as {{ill|kabuki|ja|芝居|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kabuki|qq=}} performances or shopping.
Since Yabuiri was closely tied to the New Year and Obon festivals, it continued to be observed after the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], even after the [[:en:Lunar calendar|Japanese calendar]] was replaced by the [[:en:Solar calendar|Solar calendar]]. As Japan industrialized, the number of workers increased, making Yabuiri an even more significant event. On Yabuiri, bustling districts such as [[:en:Asakusa|Asakusa]] were crowded with apprentices and workers enjoying their rare day off. The growing popularity of [[:en:Film|motion pictures]] was also fueled by Yabuiri<ref name="koubunkan" />.
After [[:en:World War II|World War II]], changes in labor laws such as the strengthening of the [[:en:Labor Standards Act (Japan)|Labour Standards Act]] and the adoption of the {{ill|five-day workweek|ja|週休2日制|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/five-day workweek|qq=|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=休日}} led to the decline of Yabuiri. Instead, it was absorbed into the longer {{ill|New Year holidays|ja|正月休み|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Year holidays|qq=|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=正月}} and {{ill|Obon holidays|ja|盆休み|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Obon holidays|qq=|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=お盆}}, surviving mainly as the tradition of {{ill|returning home for the holidays|ja|帰省|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/returning home for the holidays|qq=}}.
== Etymology and Cultural Influence == The origin of the term ''Yabuiri'' is unclear, and several theories exist. One theory suggests that the name comes from the idea of returning to a "deep thicket" (藪, ''yabu'') in the countryside. Another suggests that it is derived from a corruption of ''Yado-iri'' (宿入り), meaning "returning to one's lodging"<ref name="koubunkan" /><ref>「日本人のしきたり」p55 飯倉晴武著 青春出版社 2003rd year 1月25日1版発行</ref>. A similar term, ''Yado-sagari'' (宿下がり), was used to describe women of the [[:en:Ōoku|Ōoku]] returning home.
Yabuiri was a rare and precious holiday for servants, making it a major event in their lives. As a result, the custom was frequently referenced in literature, [[:en:Haiku|haiku]], and [[:en:Rakugo|Rakugo]] stories such as {{ill|Yabuiri (Rakugo)|ja|藪入り|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yabuiri (Rakugo)|qq=}}, formerly known as {{ill|Okama-sama|ja|お釜さま|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okama-sama|qq=}}.
== Related Topics == * [[:en:Boxing Day|Boxing Day]]

A similar term, ''Yado-sagari'' (宿下がり), was used to describe women of the [[:en:Ōoku|Ōoku]] returning home.
Yabuiri was a rare and precious holiday for servants, making it a major event in their lives. As a result, the custom was frequently referenced in literature, [[:en:Haiku|haiku]], and [[:en:Rakugo|Rakugo]] stories such as {{ill|Yabuiri (Rakugo)|ja|藪入り|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yabuiri (Rakugo)|qq=}}, formerly known as {{ill|Okama-sama|ja|お釜さま|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okama-sama|qq=}}.

[[sn:Yabuiri]] [[ja:藪入り]] [[sn:藪入り]]
[[ar:Yabuiri]] [[Category:Seasonal terms for New Year]] [[Category:Annual events in Japan]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[Category:pages without english wikidata name]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Yabuiri]][[Category:qq]]

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11625091]] Wikidata name: [[Category:Add wikidata]] [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Lunisolar July}} {{Lunisolar January}} {{Otheruses|Yabuiri|落語|{{ill|Yabuiri (Rakugo)|ja|藪入り (落語)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yabuiri (Rakugo)|qq=|1=Yabuiri (Rakugo)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11625090}}}}
'''Yabuiri''' (藪入り) or '''Yabu-iri''' was a traditional holiday in Japan during which {{ill|apprentices|ja|丁稚|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/apprentices|qq=|1=apprentice|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11353322}} and {{ill|maids|ja|女中|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/maids|qq=|1=jochū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11446281}} who worked as live-in servants in {{ill|merchant houses|ja|商家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/merchant houses|qq=}} were allowed to return home to visit their families. The holiday was observed on {{ill|Lunisolar January 16|ja|1月16日 (旧暦)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lunisolar January 16|qq=|1=16th day of the 1st month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q839090}} and {{ill|Lunisolar July 16|ja|7月16日 (旧暦)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lunisolar July 16|qq=|1=16th day of the 7th month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q839688}}. The July observance was also called "{{ill|Later Yabuiri|ja|後の藪入り|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Later Yabuiri|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" (''Nochi no Yabuiri'')<ref>{{kotobank|後の藪入り}}</ref>.
== Origins == The custom of Yabuiri became widespread among urban merchant families during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. It is believed to have originally been a day when a {{ill|bride|ja|嫁|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bride|qq=|1=bride|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8194100}} in a {{ill|virilocal marriage|ja|嫁取り婚|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/virilocal marriage|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} would return to her parents' home. However, as urbanization progressed, it evolved into a custom for apprentices and domestic workers<ref name="heibon">「国民百科事典7」平凡社 p292-293 1962nd year 6月15日初版発行</ref>.
In some regions, particularly in {{ill|Kansai|ja|関西地方|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai|qq=|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=関西|1=Kansai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1111292}} and [[:en:Kagoshima (disambiguation)|Kagoshima]], Yabuiri was known as '''O-yagenzo''' ('''親見参''', "Parent Visitation")<ref name="heibon" /><ref name="koubunkan">「日本民俗大辞典 下」p737 吉川弘文館 2000th year 3月10日1版発行</ref>. Since the holiday was observed on days ending in six, it was also referred to as '''Rokuiri''' ('''六入り''', "Six-entry") in Kansai<ref name="koubunkan" />.
The dates of {{ill|Lunisolar January 15|ja|1月15日 (旧暦)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lunisolar January 15|qq=|1=15th day of the 1st month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q838395}} ([[:en:First Full Moon Festival|Koshōgatsu]], the "Little New Year") and {{ill|Lunisolar July 15|ja|7月15日 (旧暦)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lunisolar July 15|qq=|1=15th day of the 7th month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q839745}} ([[:en:Obon|Obon]], the Festival of the Dead) were important festival days. The timing of Yabuiri allowed apprentices and maids to complete their duties at their workplaces before returning home to take part in family festivities<ref name="koubunkan" />. Over time, these dates also became associated with the belief that {{ill|Enma Daio|ja|閻魔大王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enma Daio|qq=|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=閻魔|1=Yama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q45097}}, the King of Hell, would cease punishing the souls of the dead on these days. As a result, many {{ill|Enma halls|ja|閻魔堂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enma halls|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Jūō halls|ja|十王堂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jūō halls|qq=|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=正覚寺 (奈良市西紀寺町)|1=Shōgaku-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q22119006}} would open their doors for special ceremonies, and [[:en:Ennichi|temple fairs]] would be held<ref name="koubunkan" />.
== Evolution == On Yabuiri, the master of the household would give his servants gifts such as new {{ill|kimono|ja|着物|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kimono|qq=|1=kimono|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q132223862}}, [[:en:Footwear|footwear]], spending money, and souvenirs to take home<ref name="koubunkan" />. The servants would then return to their family homes to spend the day with their parents. For those who had traveled long distances for work or had reached adulthood and could not return home, the day was spent enjoying entertainment such as {{ill|kabuki|ja|芝居|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kabuki|qq=|1=kabuki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q15096475}} performances or shopping.
Since Yabuiri was closely tied to the New Year and Obon festivals, it continued to be observed after the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], even after the [[:en:Lunar calendar|Japanese calendar]] was replaced by the [[:en:Solar calendar|Solar calendar]]. As Japan industrialized, the number of workers increased, making Yabuiri an even more significant event. On Yabuiri, bustling districts such as [[:en:Asakusa|Asakusa]] were crowded with apprentices and workers enjoying their rare day off. The growing popularity of [[:en:Film|motion pictures]] was also fueled by Yabuiri<ref name="koubunkan" />.
After [[:en:World War II|World War II]], changes in labor laws such as the strengthening of the [[:en:Labor Standards Act (Japan)|Labour Standards Act]] and the adoption of the {{ill|five-day workweek|ja|週休2日制|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/five-day workweek|qq=|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=休日|1=non-working day|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2174956}} led to the decline of Yabuiri. Instead, it was absorbed into the longer {{ill|New Year holidays|ja|正月休み|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Year holidays|qq=|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=正月|1=Japanese New Year|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q701398}} and {{ill|Obon holidays|ja|盆休み|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Obon holidays|qq=|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=お盆|1=Bon Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1154888}}, surviving mainly as the tradition of {{ill|returning home for the holidays|ja|帰省|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/returning home for the holidays|qq=|1=returning home for the holidays|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q124499736}}.
== Etymology and Cultural Influence == The origin of the term ''Yabuiri'' is unclear, and several theories exist. One theory suggests that the name comes from the idea of returning to a "deep thicket" (藪, ''yabu'') in the countryside. Another suggests that it is derived from a corruption of ''Yado-iri'' (宿入り), meaning "returning to one's lodging"<ref name="koubunkan" /><ref>「日本人のしきたり」p55 飯倉晴武著 青春出版社 2003rd year 1月25日1版発行</ref>. A similar term, ''Yado-sagari'' (宿下がり), was used to describe women of the [[:en:Ōoku|Ōoku]] returning home.
Yabuiri was a rare and precious holiday for servants, making it a major event in their lives. As a result, the custom was frequently referenced in literature, [[:en:Haiku|haiku]], and [[:en:Rakugo|Rakugo]] stories such as {{ill|Yabuiri (Rakugo)|ja|藪入り|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yabuiri (Rakugo)|qq=|1=Yabuiri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11625091}}, formerly known as {{ill|Okama-sama|ja|お釜さま|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okama-sama|qq=|1=Okama-sama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11263495}}.

It is believed to have originally been a day when a {{ill|bride|ja|嫁|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bride|qq=|1=bride|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8194100}} in a {{ill|virilocal marriage|ja|嫁取り婚|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/virilocal marriage|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} would return to her parents' home. However, as urbanization progressed, it evolved into a custom for apprentices and domestic workers<ref name="heibon">「国民百科事典7」平凡社 p292-293 1962nd year 6月15日初版発行</ref>.
In some regions, particularly in {{ill|Kansai|ja|関西地方|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai|qq=|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=関西|1=Kansai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1111292}} and [[:en:Kagoshima (disambiguation)|Kagoshima]], Yabuiri was known as '''O-yagenzo''' ('''親見参''', "Parent Visitation")<ref name="heibon" /><ref name="koubunkan">「日本民俗大辞典 下」p737 吉川弘文館 2000th year 3月10日1版発行</ref>. Since the holiday was observed on days ending in six, it was also referred to as '''Rokuiri''' ('''六入り''', "Six-entry") in Kansai<ref name="koubunkan" />.
The dates of {{ill|Lunisolar January 15|ja|1月15日 (旧暦)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lunisolar January 15|qq=|1=15th day of the 1st month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q838395}} ([[:en:First Full Moon Festival|Koshōgatsu]], the "Little New Year") and {{ill|Lunisolar July 15|ja|7月15日 (旧暦)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lunisolar July 15|qq=|1=15th day of the 7th month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q839745}} ([[:en:Obon|Obon]], the Festival of the Dead) were important festival days. The timing of Yabuiri allowed apprentices and maids to complete their duties at their workplaces before returning home to take part in family festivities<ref name="koubunkan" />. Over time, these dates also became associated with the belief that {{ill|Enma Daio|ja|閻魔大王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enma Daio|qq=|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=閻魔|1=Yama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q45097}}, the King of Hell, would cease punishing the souls of the dead on these days. As a result, many {{ill|Enma halls|ja|閻魔堂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enma halls|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Jūō halls|ja|十王堂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jūō halls|qq=|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=正覚寺 (奈良市西紀寺町)|1=Shōgaku-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q22119006}} would open their doors for special ceremonies, and [[:en:Ennichi|temple fairs]] would be held<ref name="koubunkan" />.
== Evolution == On Yabuiri, the master of the household would give his servants gifts such as new {{ill|kimono|ja|着物|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kimono|qq=|1=kimono|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q132223862}}, [[:en:Footwear|footwear]], spending money, and souvenirs to take home<ref name="koubunkan" />. The servants would then return to their family homes to spend the day with their parents. For those who had traveled long distances for work or had reached adulthood and could not return home, the day was spent enjoying entertainment such as {{ill|kabuki|ja|芝居|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kabuki|qq=|1=kabuki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q15096475}} performances or shopping.
Since Yabuiri was closely tied to the New Year and Obon festivals, it continued to be observed after the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], even after the [[:en:Lunar calendar|Japanese calendar]] was replaced by the [[:en:Solar calendar|Solar calendar]]. As Japan industrialized, the number of workers increased, making Yabuiri an even more significant event. On Yabuiri, bustling districts such as [[:en:Asakusa|Asakusa]] were crowded with apprentices and workers enjoying their rare day off. The growing popularity of [[:en:Film|motion pictures]] was also fueled by Yabuiri<ref name="koubunkan" />.
After [[:en:World War II|World War II]], changes in labor laws such as the strengthening of the [[:en:Labor Standards Act (Japan)|Labour Standards Act]] and the adoption of the {{ill|five-day workweek|ja|週休2日制|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/five-day workweek|qq=|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=休日|1=non-working day|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2174956}} led to the decline of Yabuiri. Instead, it was absorbed into the longer {{ill|New Year holidays|ja|正月休み|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Year holidays|qq=|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=正月|1=Japanese New Year|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q701398}} and {{ill|Obon holidays|ja|盆休み|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Obon holidays|qq=|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=お盆|1=Bon Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1154888}}, surviving mainly as the tradition of {{ill|returning home for the holidays|ja|帰省|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/returning home for the holidays|qq=|1=returning home for the holidays|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q124499736}}.
== Etymology and Cultural Influence == The origin of the term ''Yabuiri'' is unclear, and several theories exist. One theory suggests that the name comes from the idea of returning to a "deep thicket" (藪, ''yabu'') in the countryside. Another suggests that it is derived from a corruption of ''Yado-iri'' (宿入り), meaning "returning to one's lodging"<ref name="koubunkan" /><ref>「日本人のしきたり」p55 飯倉晴武著 青春出版社 2003rd year 1月25日1版発行</ref>. A similar term, ''Yado-sagari'' (宿下がり), was used to describe women of the [[:en:Ōoku|Ōoku]] returning home.
Yabuiri was a rare and precious holiday for servants, making it a major event in their lives. As a result, the custom was frequently referenced in literature, [[:en:Haiku|haiku]], and [[:en:Rakugo|Rakugo]] stories such as {{ill|Yabuiri (Rakugo)|ja|藪入り|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yabuiri (Rakugo)|qq=|1=Yabuiri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11625091}}, formerly known as {{ill|Okama-sama|ja|お釜さま|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okama-sama|qq=|1=Okama-sama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11263495}}.

'''Yabuiri''' (藪入り) or '''Yabu-iri''' was a traditional holiday in Japan during which {{ill|apprentices|ja|丁稚|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/apprentices|qq=|1=apprentice|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11353322}} and {{ill|maids|ja|女中|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/maids|qq=|1=jochū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11446281}} who worked as live-in servants in {{ill|merchant houses|ja|商家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/merchant houses|qq=}} were allowed to return home to visit their families. The holiday was observed on {{ill|Lunisolar January 16|ja|1月16日 (旧暦)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lunisolar January 16|qq=|1=16th day of the 1st month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q839090}} and {{ill|Lunisolar July 16|ja|7月16日 (旧暦)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lunisolar July 16|qq=|1=16th day of the 7th month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q839688}}. The July observance was also called "{{ill|Later Yabuiri|ja|後の藪入り|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Later Yabuiri|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" (''Nochi no Yabuiri'')<ref>{{kotobank|後の藪入り}}</ref>.

It is believed to have originally been a day when a {{ill|bride|ja|嫁|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bride|qq=|1=bride|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8194100}} in a {{ill|virilocal marriage|ja|嫁取り婚|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/virilocal marriage|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} would return to her parents' home. However, as urbanization progressed, it evolved into a custom for apprentices and domestic workers<ref name="heibon">「国民百科事典7」平凡社 p292-293 1962nd year 6月15日初版発行</ref>.
In some regions, particularly in {{ill|Kansai|ja|関西地方|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai|qq=|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=関西|1=Kansai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1111292}} and [[:en:Kagoshima (disambiguation)|Kagoshima]], Yabuiri was known as '''O-yagenzo''' ('''親見参''', "Parent Visitation")<ref name="heibon" /><ref name="koubunkan">「日本民俗大辞典 下」p737 吉川弘文館 2000th year 3月10日1版発行</ref>. Since the holiday was observed on days ending in six, it was also referred to as '''Rokuiri''' ('''六入り''', "Six-entry") in Kansai<ref name="koubunkan" />.
The dates of {{ill|Lunisolar January 15|ja|1月15日 (旧暦)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lunisolar January 15|qq=|1=15th day of the 1st month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q838395}} ([[:en:First Full Moon Festival|Koshōgatsu]], the "Little New Year") and {{ill|Lunisolar July 15|ja|7月15日 (旧暦)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lunisolar July 15|qq=|1=15th day of the 7th month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q839745}} ([[:en:Obon|Obon]], the Festival of the Dead) were important festival days. The timing of Yabuiri allowed apprentices and maids to complete their duties at their workplaces before returning home to take part in family festivities<ref name="koubunkan" />. Over time, these dates also became associated with the belief that {{ill|Enma Daio|ja|閻魔大王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enma Daio|qq=|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=閻魔|1=Yama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q45097}}, the King of Hell, would cease punishing the souls of the dead on these days. As a result, many {{ill|Enma halls|ja|閻魔堂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enma halls|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Jūō halls|ja|十王堂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jūō halls|qq=|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=正覚寺 (奈良市西紀寺町)|1=Shōgaku-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q22119006}} would open their doors for special ceremonies, and [[:en:Ennichi|temple fairs]] would be held<ref name="koubunkan" />.

For those who had traveled long distances for work or had reached adulthood and could not return home, the day was spent enjoying entertainment such as {{ill|kabuki|ja|芝居|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kabuki|qq=|1=kabuki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q15096475}} performances or shopping.

As Japan industrialized, the number of workers increased, making Yabuiri an even more significant event. On Yabuiri, bustling districts such as [[:en:Asakusa|Asakusa]] were crowded with apprentices and workers enjoying their rare day off. On Yabuiri, bustling districts such as [[:en:Asakusa|Asakusa]] were crowded with apprentices and workers enjoying their rare day off. The growing popularity of [[:en:Film|motion pictures]] was also fueled by Yabuiri<ref name="koubunkan" />.

After [[:en:World War II|World War II]], changes in labor laws such as the strengthening of the [[:en:Labor Standards Act (Japan)|Labour Standards Act]] and the adoption of the {{ill|five-day workweek|ja|週休2日制|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/five-day workweek|qq=|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=休日|1=non-working day|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2174956}} led to the decline of Yabuiri. Instead, it was absorbed into the longer {{ill|New Year holidays|ja|正月休み|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Year holidays|qq=|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=正月|1=Japanese New Year|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q701398}} and {{ill|Obon holidays|ja|盆休み|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Obon holidays|qq=|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=お盆|1=Bon Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1154888}}, surviving mainly as the tradition of {{ill|returning home for the holidays|ja|帰省|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/returning home for the holidays|qq=|1=returning home for the holidays|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q124499736}}.

== Etymology and Cultural Influence ==
The origin of the term ''Yabuiri'' is unclear, and several theories exist. One theory suggests that the name comes from the idea of returning to a "deep thicket" (藪, ''yabu'') in the countryside. Another suggests that it is derived from a corruption of ''Yado-iri'' (宿入り), meaning "returning to one's lodging"<ref name="koubunkan" /><ref>「日本人のしきたり」p55　飯倉晴武著　青春出版社 2003rd year 1月25日1版発行</ref>. A similar term, ''Yado-sagari'' (宿下がり), was used to describe women of the [[:en:Ōoku|Ōoku]] returning home.

Yabuiri was a rare and precious holiday for servants, making it a major event in their lives. As a result, the custom was frequently referenced in literature, [[:en:Haiku|haiku]], and [[:en:Rakugo|Rakugo]] stories such as {{ill|Yabuiri (Rakugo)|ja|藪入り|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yabuiri (Rakugo)|qq=|1=Yabuiri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11625091}}, formerly known as {{ill|Okama-sama|ja|お釜さま|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okama-sama|qq=|1=Okama-sama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11263495}}.

{{Otheruses|Yabuiri|落語|{{ill|Yabuiri (Rakugo)|ja|藪入り (落語)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yabuiri (Rakugo)|1=Yabuiri (Rakugo)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11625090}}}}

'''Yabuiri''' (藪入り) or '''Yabu-iri''' was a traditional holiday in Japan during which {{ill|apprentices|ja|丁稚|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/apprentices|1=apprentice|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11353322}} and {{ill|maids|ja|女中|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/maids|1=jochū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11446281}} who worked as live-in servants in {{ill|merchant houses|ja|商家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/merchant houses|qq=}} were allowed to return home to visit their families. The holiday was observed on {{ill|Lunisolar January 16|ja|1月16日 (旧暦)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lunisolar January 16|1=16th day of the 1st month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q839090}} and {{ill|Lunisolar July 16|ja|7月16日 (旧暦)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lunisolar July 16|1=16th day of the 7th month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q839688}}. The July observance was also called "{{ill|Later Yabuiri|ja|後の藪入り|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Later Yabuiri|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" (''Nochi no Yabuiri'')<ref>{{kotobank|後の藪入り}}</ref>.

== Origins ==
The custom of Yabuiri became widespread among urban merchant families during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. It is believed to have originally been a day when a {{ill|bride|ja|嫁|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bride|1=bride|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8194100}} in a {{ill|virilocal marriage|ja|嫁取り婚|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/virilocal marriage|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} would return to her parents' home. However, as urbanization progressed, it evolved into a custom for apprentices and domestic workers<ref name="heibon">「国民百科事典7」平凡社　p292-293　1962nd year 6月15日初版発行</ref>. 

In some regions, particularly in {{ill|Kansai|ja|関西地方|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=関西|1=Kansai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1111292}} and [[:en:Kagoshima (disambiguation)|Kagoshima]], Yabuiri was known as '''O-yagenzo''' ('''親見参''', "Parent Visitation")<ref name="heibon" /><ref name="koubunkan">「日本民俗大辞典　下」p737　吉川弘文館 2000th year 3月10日1版発行</ref>. Since the holiday was observed on days ending in six, it was also referred to as '''Rokuiri''' ('''六入り''', "Six-entry") in Kansai<ref name="koubunkan" />.

The dates of {{ill|Lunisolar January 15|ja|1月15日 (旧暦)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lunisolar January 15|1=15th day of the 1st month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q838395}} ([[:en:First Full Moon Festival|Koshōgatsu]], the "Little New Year") and {{ill|Lunisolar July 15|ja|7月15日 (旧暦)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lunisolar July 15|1=15th day of the 7th month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q839745}} ([[:en:Obon|Obon]], the Festival of the Dead) were important festival days. The timing of Yabuiri allowed apprentices and maids to complete their duties at their workplaces before returning home to take part in family festivities<ref name="koubunkan" />. Over time, these dates also became associated with the belief that {{ill|Enma Daio|ja|閻魔大王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enma Daio|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=閻魔|1=Yama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q45097}}, the King of Hell, would cease punishing the souls of the dead on these days. As a result, many {{ill|Enma halls|ja|閻魔堂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enma halls|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Jūō halls|ja|十王堂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jūō halls|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=正覚寺 (奈良市西紀寺町)|1=Shōgaku-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q22119006}} would open their doors for special ceremonies, and [[:en:Ennichi|temple fairs]] would be held<ref name="koubunkan" />.

== Evolution ==
On Yabuiri, the master of the household would give his servants gifts such as new {{ill|kimono|ja|着物|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kimono|1=kimono|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q132223862}}, [[:en:Footwear|footwear]], spending money, and souvenirs to take home<ref name="koubunkan" />. The servants would then return to their family homes to spend the day with their parents. For those who had traveled long distances for work or had reached adulthood and could not return home, the day was spent enjoying entertainment such as {{ill|kabuki|ja|芝居|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kabuki|1=kabuki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q15096475}} performances or shopping.

Since Yabuiri was closely tied to the New Year and Obon festivals, it continued to be observed after the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], even after the [[:en:Lunar calendar|Japanese calendar]] was replaced by the [[:en:Solar calendar|Solar calendar]]. As Japan industrialized, the number of workers increased, making Yabuiri an even more significant event. On Yabuiri, bustling districts such as [[:en:Asakusa|Asakusa]] were crowded with apprentices and workers enjoying their rare day off. The growing popularity of [[:en:Film|motion pictures]] was also fueled by Yabuiri<ref name="koubunkan" />.

After [[:en:World War II|World War II]], changes in labor laws such as the strengthening of the [[:en:Labor Standards Act (Japan)|Labour Standards Act]] and the adoption of the {{ill|five-day workweek|ja|週休2日制|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/five-day workweek|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=休日|1=non-working day|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2174956}} led to the decline of Yabuiri. Instead, it was absorbed into the longer {{ill|New Year holidays|ja|正月休み|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Year holidays|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=正月|1=Japanese New Year|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q701398}} and {{ill|Obon holidays|ja|盆休み|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Obon holidays|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=お盆|1=Bon Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1154888}}, surviving mainly as the tradition of {{ill|returning home for the holidays|ja|帰省|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/returning home for the holidays|1=returning home for the holidays|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q124499736}}.

== Etymology and Cultural Influence ==
The origin of the term ''Yabuiri'' is unclear, and several theories exist. One theory suggests that the name comes from the idea of returning to a "deep thicket" (藪, ''yabu'') in the countryside. Another suggests that it is derived from a corruption of ''Yado-iri'' (宿入り), meaning "returning to one's lodging"<ref name="koubunkan" /><ref>「日本人のしきたり」p55　飯倉晴武著　青春出版社 2003rd year 1月25日1版発行</ref>. A similar term, ''Yado-sagari'' (宿下がり), was used to describe women of the [[:en:Ōoku|Ōoku]] returning home.

Yabuiri was a rare and precious holiday for servants, making it a major event in their lives. As a result, the custom was frequently referenced in literature, [[:en:Haiku|haiku]], and [[:en:Rakugo|Rakugo]] stories such as {{ill|Yabuiri (Rakugo)|ja|藪入り|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yabuiri (Rakugo)|1=Yabuiri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11625091}}, formerly known as {{ill|Okama-sama|ja|お釜さま|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okama-sama|1=Okama-sama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11263495}}.

'''Yabuiri''' (藪入り) or '''Yabu-iri''' was a traditional holiday in Japan during which {{ill|apprentices|ja|丁稚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/apprentices|1=apprentice|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11353322}} and {{ill|maids|ja|女中|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/maids|1=jochū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11446281}} who worked as live-in servants in {{ill|merchant houses|ja|商家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/merchant houses|qq=}} were allowed to return home to visit their families. The holiday was observed on {{ill|Lunisolar January 16|ja|1月16日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lunisolar January 16|1=16th day of the 1st month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q839090}} and {{ill|Lunisolar July 16|ja|7月16日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lunisolar July 16|1=16th day of the 7th month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q839688}}. The July observance was also called "{{ill|Later Yabuiri|ja|後の藪入り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Later Yabuiri|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" (''Nochi no Yabuiri'')<ref>{{kotobank|後の藪入り}}</ref>.

== Origins ==
The custom of Yabuiri became widespread among urban merchant families during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. It is believed to have originally been a day when a {{ill|bride|ja|嫁|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bride|1=bride|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8194100}} in a {{ill|virilocal marriage|ja|嫁取り婚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/virilocal marriage|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} would return to her parents' home. However, as urbanization progressed, it evolved into a custom for apprentices and domestic workers<ref name="heibon">「国民百科事典7」平凡社　p292-293　1962nd year 6月15日初版発行</ref>. 

In some regions, particularly in {{ill|Kansai|ja|関西地方|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=関西|1=Kansai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1111292}} and [[:en:Kagoshima (disambiguation)|Kagoshima]], Yabuiri was known as '''O-yagenzo''' ('''親見参''', "Parent Visitation")<ref name="heibon" /><ref name="koubunkan">「日本民俗大辞典　下」p737　吉川弘文館 2000th year 3月10日1版発行</ref>. Since the holiday was observed on days ending in six, it was also referred to as '''Rokuiri''' ('''六入り''', "Six-entry") in Kansai<ref name="koubunkan" />.

The dates of {{ill|Lunisolar January 15|ja|1月15日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lunisolar January 15|1=15th day of the 1st month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q838395}} ([[:en:First Full Moon Festival|Koshōgatsu]], the "Little New Year") and {{ill|Lunisolar July 15|ja|7月15日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lunisolar July 15|1=15th day of the 7th month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q839745}} ([[:en:Obon|Obon]], the Festival of the Dead) were important festival days. The timing of Yabuiri allowed apprentices and maids to complete their duties at their workplaces before returning home to take part in family festivities<ref name="koubunkan" />. Over time, these dates also became associated with the belief that {{ill|Enma Daio|ja|閻魔大王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enma Daio|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=閻魔|1=Yama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q45097}}, the King of Hell, would cease punishing the souls of the dead on these days. As a result, many {{ill|Enma halls|ja|閻魔堂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enma halls|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Jūō halls|ja|十王堂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jūō halls|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=正覚寺 (奈良市西紀寺町)|1=Shōgaku-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q22119006}} would open their doors for special ceremonies, and [[:en:Ennichi|temple fairs]] would be held<ref name="koubunkan" />.

== Evolution ==
On Yabuiri, the master of the household would give his servants gifts such as new {{ill|kimono|ja|着物|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kimono|1=kimono|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q132223862}}, [[:en:Footwear|footwear]], spending money, and souvenirs to take home<ref name="koubunkan" />. The servants would then return to their family homes to spend the day with their parents. For those who had traveled long distances for work or had reached adulthood and could not return home, the day was spent enjoying entertainment such as {{ill|kabuki|ja|芝居|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kabuki|1=kabuki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q15096475}} performances or shopping.

Since Yabuiri was closely tied to the New Year and Obon festivals, it continued to be observed after the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], even after the [[:en:Lunar calendar|Japanese calendar]] was replaced by the [[:en:Solar calendar|Solar calendar]]. As Japan industrialized, the number of workers increased, making Yabuiri an even more significant event. On Yabuiri, bustling districts such as [[:en:Asakusa|Asakusa]] were crowded with apprentices and workers enjoying their rare day off. The growing popularity of [[:en:Film|motion pictures]] was also fueled by Yabuiri<ref name="koubunkan" />.

After [[:en:World War II|World War II]], changes in labor laws such as the strengthening of the [[:en:Labor Standards Act (Japan)|Labour Standards Act]] and the adoption of the {{ill|five-day workweek|ja|週休2日制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/five-day workweek|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=休日|1=non-working day|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2174956}} led to the decline of Yabuiri. Instead, it was absorbed into the longer {{ill|New Year holidays|ja|正月休み|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Year holidays|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=正月|1=Japanese New Year|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q701398}} and {{ill|Obon holidays|ja|盆休み|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Obon holidays|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=お盆|1=Bon Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1154888}}, surviving mainly as the tradition of {{ill|returning home for the holidays|ja|帰省|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/returning home for the holidays|1=returning home for the holidays|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q124499736}}.

== Etymology and Cultural Influence ==
The origin of the term ''Yabuiri'' is unclear, and several theories exist. One theory suggests that the name comes from the idea of returning to a "deep thicket" (藪, ''yabu'') in the countryside. Another suggests that it is derived from a corruption of ''Yado-iri'' (宿入り), meaning "returning to one's lodging"<ref name="koubunkan" /><ref>「日本人のしきたり」p55　飯倉晴武著　青春出版社 2003rd year 1月25日1版発行</ref>. A similar term, ''Yado-sagari'' (宿下がり), was used to describe women of the [[:en:Ōoku|Ōoku]] returning home.

Yabuiri was a rare and precious holiday for servants, making it a major event in their lives. As a result, the custom was frequently referenced in literature, [[:en:Haiku|haiku]], and [[:en:Rakugo|Rakugo]] stories such as {{ill|Yabuiri (Rakugo)|ja|藪入り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yabuiri (Rakugo)|1=Yabuiri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11625091}}, formerly known as {{ill|Okama-sama|ja|お釜さま|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okama-sama|1=Okama-sama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11263495}}.

Instead, it was absorbed into the longer {{ill|New Year holidays|ja|正月休み|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Year holidays|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=正月|1=Japanese New Year|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q701398}} and {{ill|Obon holidays|ja|盆休み|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Obon holidays|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=お盆|1=Bon Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1154888}}, surviving mainly as the tradition of {{ill|returning home for the holidays|ja|帰省|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/returning home for the holidays|1=returning home for the holidays|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q124499736}}.

== Etymology and Cultural Influence ==
The origin of the term ''Yabuiri'' is unclear, and several theories exist. One theory suggests that the name comes from the idea of returning to a "deep thicket" (藪, ''yabu'') in the countryside. Another suggests that it is derived from a corruption of ''Yado-iri'' (宿入り), meaning "returning to one's lodging"<ref name="koubunkan" /><ref>「日本人のしきたり」p55　飯倉晴武著　青春出版社 2003rd year 1月25日1版発行</ref>. A similar term, ''Yado-sagari'' (宿下がり), was used to describe women of the [[:en:Ōoku|Ōoku]] returning home.

Yabuiri was a rare and precious holiday for servants, making it a major event in their lives. As a result, the custom was frequently referenced in literature, [[:en:Haiku|haiku]], and [[:en:Rakugo|Rakugo]] stories such as {{ill|Yabuiri (Rakugo)|ja|藪入り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yabuiri (Rakugo)|1=Yabuiri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11625091}}, formerly known as {{ill|Okama-sama|ja|お釜さま|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okama-sama|1=Okama-sama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11263495}}.

{{Lunisolar January}}
{{Otheruses|Yabuiri|落語|{{ill|Yabuiri (Rakugo)|ja|藪入り (落語)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yabuiri (Rakugo)|1=Yabuiri (Rakugo)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11625090}}}}

'''Yabuiri''' (藪入り) or '''Yabu-iri''' was a traditional holiday in Japan during which {{ill|apprentices|ja|丁稚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/apprentices|1=apprentice|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11353322}} and {{ill|maids|ja|女中|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/maids|1=jochū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11446281}} who worked as live-in servants in {{ill|merchant houses|ja|商家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/merchant houses|qq=}} were allowed to return home to visit their families. The holiday was observed on {{ill|Lunisolar January 16|ja|1月16日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lunisolar January 16|1=16th day of the 1st month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q839090}} and {{ill|Lunisolar July 16|ja|7月16日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lunisolar July 16|1=16th day of the 7th month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q839688}}. The July observance was also called "{{ill|Later Yabuiri|ja|後の藪入り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Later Yabuiri|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" (''Nochi no Yabuiri'')<ref>{{kotobank|後の藪入り}}</ref>.

== Origins ==
The custom of Yabuiri became widespread among urban merchant families during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. It is believed to have originally been a day when a {{ill|bride|ja|嫁|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bride|1=bride|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8194100}} in a {{ill|virilocal marriage|ja|嫁取り婚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/virilocal marriage|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} would return to her parents' home. However, as urbanization progressed, it evolved into a custom for apprentices and domestic workers<ref name="heibon">「国民百科事典7」平凡社　p292-293　1962nd year 6月15日初版発行</ref>. 

In some regions, particularly in {{ill|Kansai|ja|関西地方|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=関西|1=Kansai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1111292}} and [[:en:Kagoshima (disambiguation)|Kagoshima]], Yabuiri was known as '''O-yagenzo''' ('''親見参''', "Parent Visitation")<ref name="heibon" /><ref name="koubunkan">「日本民俗大辞典　下」p737　吉川弘文館 2000th year 3月10日1版発行</ref>. Since the holiday was observed on days ending in six, it was also referred to as '''Rokuiri''' ('''六入り''', "Six-entry") in Kansai<ref name="koubunkan" />.

The dates of {{ill|Lunisolar January 15|ja|1月15日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lunisolar January 15|1=15th day of the 1st month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q838395}} ([[:en:First Full Moon Festival|Koshōgatsu]], the "Little New Year") and {{ill|Lunisolar July 15|ja|7月15日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lunisolar July 15|1=15th day of the 7th month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q839745}} ([[:en:Obon|Obon]], the Festival of the Dead) were important festival days. The timing of Yabuiri allowed apprentices and maids to complete their duties at their workplaces before returning home to take part in family festivities<ref name="koubunkan" />. Over time, these dates also became associated with the belief that {{ill|Enma Daio|ja|閻魔大王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enma Daio|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=閻魔|1=Yama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q45097}}, the King of Hell, would cease punishing the souls of the dead on these days. As a result, many {{ill|Enma halls|ja|閻魔堂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enma halls|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Jūō halls|ja|十王堂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jūō halls|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=正覚寺 (奈良市西紀寺町)|1=Shōgaku-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q22119006}} would open their doors for special ceremonies, and [[:en:Ennichi|temple fairs]] would be held<ref name="koubunkan" />.

== Evolution ==
On Yabuiri, the master of the household would give his servants gifts such as new {{ill|kimono|ja|着物|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kimono|1=kimono|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q132223862}}, [[:en:Footwear|footwear]], spending money, and souvenirs to take home<ref name="koubunkan" />. The servants would then return to their family homes to spend the day with their parents. For those who had traveled long distances for work or had reached adulthood and could not return home, the day was spent enjoying entertainment such as {{ill|kabuki|ja|芝居|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kabuki|1=kabuki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q15096475}} performances or shopping.

Since Yabuiri was closely tied to the New Year and Obon festivals, it continued to be observed after the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], even after the [[:en:Lunar calendar|Japanese calendar]] was replaced by the [[:en:Solar calendar|Solar calendar]]. As Japan industrialized, the number of workers increased, making Yabuiri an even more significant event. On Yabuiri, bustling districts such as [[:en:Asakusa|Asakusa]] were crowded with apprentices and workers enjoying their rare day off. The growing popularity of [[:en:Film|motion pictures]] was also fueled by Yabuiri<ref name="koubunkan" />.

After [[:en:World War II|World War II]], changes in labor laws such as the strengthening of the [[:en:Labor Standards Act (Japan)|Labour Standards Act]] and the adoption of the {{ill|five-day workweek|ja|週休2日制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/five-day workweek|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=休日|1=non-working day|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2174956}} led to the decline of Yabuiri. Instead, it was absorbed into the longer {{ill|New Year holidays|ja|正月休み|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Year holidays|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=正月|1=Japanese New Year|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q701398}} and {{ill|Obon holidays|ja|盆休み|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Obon holidays|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=お盆|1=Bon Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1154888}}, surviving mainly as the tradition of {{ill|returning home for the holidays|ja|帰省|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/returning home for the holidays|1=returning home for the holidays|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q124499736}}.

== Etymology and Cultural Influence ==
The origin of the term ''Yabuiri'' is unclear, and several theories exist. One theory suggests that the name comes from the idea of returning to a "deep thicket" (藪, ''yabu'') in the countryside. Another suggests that it is derived from a corruption of ''Yado-iri'' (宿入り), meaning "returning to one's lodging"<ref name="koubunkan" /><ref>「日本人のしきたり」p55　飯倉晴武著　青春出版社 2003rd year 1月25日1版発行</ref>. A similar term, ''Yado-sagari'' (宿下がり), was used to describe women of the [[:en:Ōoku|Ōoku]] returning home.

Yabuiri was a rare and precious holiday for servants, making it a major event in their lives. As a result, the custom was frequently referenced in literature, [[:en:Haiku|haiku]], and [[:en:Rakugo|Rakugo]] stories such as {{ill|Yabuiri (Rakugo)|ja|藪入り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yabuiri (Rakugo)|1=Yabuiri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11625091}}, formerly known as {{ill|Okama-sama|ja|お釜さま|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okama-sama|1=Okama-sama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11263495}}.

In some regions, particularly in {{ill|Kansai|ja|関西地方|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=関西|1=Kansai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1111292}} and [[:en:Kagoshima (disambiguation)|Kagoshima]], Yabuiri was known as '''O-yagenzo''' ('''親見参''', "Parent Visitation")<ref name="heibon" /><ref name="koubunkan">「日本民俗大辞典　下」p737　吉川弘文館 2000th year 3月10日1版発行</ref>. Since the holiday was observed on days ending in six, it was also referred to as '''Rokuiri''' ('''六入り''', "Six-entry") in Kansai<ref name="koubunkan" />.

The dates of {{ill|Lunisolar January 15|ja|1月15日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lunisolar January 15|1=15th day of the 1st month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q838395}} ([[:en:First Full Moon Festival|Koshōgatsu]], the "Little New Year") and {{ill|Lunisolar July 15|ja|7月15日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lunisolar July 15|1=15th day of the 7th month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q839745}} ([[:en:Obon|Obon]], the Festival of the Dead) were important festival days. The timing of Yabuiri allowed apprentices and maids to complete their duties at their workplaces before returning home to take part in family festivities<ref name="koubunkan" />. Over time, these dates also became associated with the belief that {{ill|Enma Daio|ja|閻魔大王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enma Daio|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=閻魔|1=Yama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q45097}}, the King of Hell, would cease punishing the souls of the dead on these days. As a result, many {{ill|Enma halls|ja|閻魔堂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enma halls|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Jūō halls|ja|十王堂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jūō halls|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=正覚寺 (奈良市西紀寺町)|1=Shōgaku-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q22119006}} would open their doors for special ceremonies, and [[:en:Ennichi|temple fairs]] would be held<ref name="koubunkan" />.

== Evolution ==
On Yabuiri, the master of the household would give his servants gifts such as new {{ill|kimono|ja|着物|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kimono|1=kimono|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q132223862}}, [[:en:Footwear|footwear]], spending money, and souvenirs to take home<ref name="koubunkan" />. The servants would then return to their family homes to spend the day with their parents. For those who had traveled long distances for work or had reached adulthood and could not return home, the day was spent enjoying entertainment such as {{ill|kabuki|ja|芝居|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kabuki|1=kabuki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q15096475}} performances or shopping.

Since Yabuiri was closely tied to the New Year and Obon festivals, it continued to be observed after the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], even after the [[:en:Lunar calendar|Japanese calendar]] was replaced by the [[:en:Solar calendar|Solar calendar]]. As Japan industrialized, the number of workers increased, making Yabuiri an even more significant event. On Yabuiri, bustling districts such as [[:en:Asakusa|Asakusa]] were crowded with apprentices and workers enjoying their rare day off. The growing popularity of [[:en:Film|motion pictures]] was also fueled by Yabuiri<ref name="koubunkan" />.

After [[:en:World War II|World War II]], changes in labor laws such as the strengthening of the [[:en:Labor Standards Act (Japan)|Labour Standards Act]] and the adoption of the {{ill|five-day workweek|ja|週休2日制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/five-day workweek|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=休日|1=non-working day|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2174956}} led to the decline of Yabuiri. Instead, it was absorbed into the longer {{ill|New Year holidays|ja|正月休み|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Year holidays|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=正月|1=Japanese New Year|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q701398}} and {{ill|Obon holidays|ja|盆休み|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Obon holidays|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=お盆|1=Bon Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1154888}}, surviving mainly as the tradition of {{ill|returning home for the holidays|ja|帰省|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/returning home for the holidays|1=returning home for the holidays|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q124499736}}.

== Etymology and Cultural Influence ==
The origin of the term ''Yabuiri'' is unclear, and several theories exist. One theory suggests that the name comes from the idea of returning to a "deep thicket" (藪, ''yabu'') in the countryside. Another suggests that it is derived from a corruption of ''Yado-iri'' (宿入り), meaning "returning to one's lodging"<ref name="koubunkan" /><ref>「日本人のしきたり」p55　飯倉晴武著　青春出版社 2003rd year 1月25日1版発行</ref>. A similar term, ''Yado-sagari'' (宿下がり), was used to describe women of the [[:en:Ōoku|Ōoku]] returning home.

Yabuiri was a rare and precious holiday for servants, making it a major event in their lives. As a result, the custom was frequently referenced in literature, [[:en:Haiku|haiku]], and [[:en:Rakugo|Rakugo]] stories such as {{ill|Yabuiri (Rakugo)|ja|藪入り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yabuiri (Rakugo)|1=Yabuiri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11625091}}, formerly known as {{ill|Okama-sama|ja|お釜さま|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okama-sama|1=Okama-sama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11263495}}.

'''Yabuiri''' (藪入り) or '''Yabu-iri''' was a traditional holiday in Japan during which {{ill|apprentices|ja|丁稚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/apprentices|1=apprentice|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11353322}} and {{ill|maids|ja|女中|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/maids|1=jochū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11446281}} who worked as live-in servants in {{ill|merchant houses|ja|商家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/merchant houses|qq=}} were allowed to return home to visit their families. The holiday was observed on {{ill|Lunisolar January 16|ja|1月16日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lunisolar January 16|1=16th day of the 1st month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q839090}} and {{ill|Lunisolar July 16|ja|7月16日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lunisolar July 16|1=16th day of the 7th month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q839688}}. The July observance was also called "{{ill|Later Yabuiri|ja|後の藪入り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Later Yabuiri|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" (''Nochi no Yabuiri'')<ref>{{kotobank|後の藪入り}}</ref>.

In some regions, particularly in {{ill|Kansai|ja|関西地方|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=関西|1=Kansai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1111292}} and [[:en:Kagoshima (disambiguation)|Kagoshima]], Yabuiri was known as '''O-yagenzo''' ('''親見参''', "Parent Visitation")<ref name="heibon" /><ref name="koubunkan">「日本民俗大辞典　下」p737　吉川弘文館 2000th year 3月10日1版発行</ref>. Over time, these dates also became associated with the belief that {{ill|Enma Daio|ja|閻魔大王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enma Daio|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=閻魔|1=Yama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q45097}}, the King of Hell, would cease punishing the souls of the dead on these days. As a result, many {{ill|Enma halls|ja|閻魔堂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enma halls|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Jūō halls|ja|十王堂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jūō halls|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=正覚寺 (奈良市西紀寺町)|1=Shōgaku-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q22119006}} would open their doors for special ceremonies, and [[:en:Ennichi|temple fairs]] would be held<ref name="koubunkan" />.

A similar term, ''Yado-sagari'' (宿下がり), was used to describe women of the [[:en:Ōoku|Ōoku]] returning home.

Yabuiri was a rare and precious holiday for servants, making it a major event in their lives. As a result, the custom was frequently referenced in literature, [[:en:Haiku|haiku]], and [[:en:Rakugo|Rakugo]] stories such as {{ill|Yabuiri (Rakugo)|ja|藪入り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yabuiri (Rakugo)|1=Yabuiri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11625091}}, formerly known as {{ill|Okama-sama|ja|お釜さま|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okama-sama|1=Okama-sama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11263495}}.
